was his mother-in-law; Donated original Stiles Hall building

narrative_blocks.header: Whitneyville to Berkeley: Gold Rush assayer father
narrative_blocks.body: Born at Whitneyville, Connecticut — the company town built around his family's Whitney Armory — Charles went west during the Gold Rush and assayed gold for Wells Fargo & Co. in San Francisco. He died in Berkeley in 1897, father of three notable children: Anson Stiles Blake, Eliza Seely Blake Thacher, and Robert Pierpont Blake.

narrative_blocks.header: Three children dead before age three, 1869-1883
narrative_blocks.body: Charles and Harriet Blake lost three of their seven children to early death across fourteen years: firstborn Jane in 1869 at eight days, Walter Whitney in 1879 at age two, and Henrietta MacWhorter in 1883 at eighteen months. Their four surviving children — Anson Stiles, Eliza Seely, Edwin Tyler, and Robert Pierpont — all reached adulthood.

narrative_blocks.header: Born and dead within eight days, August 1869
narrative_blocks.body: Jane Blake, firstborn of Charles Thompson Blake and Harriet Waters Stiles, was born in San Francisco on August 10, 1869 and died eight days later on August 18. She was the first of three Blake children to die young; her surviving four siblings were born across the next seventeen years.

notable.notable_blurb: Grand old man of Stiles Hall, UC Berkeley

education.notes: One of UC Berkeley's oldest living graduates at his death in 1959. Awarded honorary Doctor of Laws by the university.

career.organization: Blake Brothers Co. (Richmond, California — quarry firm)
University YMCA at UC Berkeley (Stiles Hall)
career.notes: Served as chairman for fifty years; declared 'Grand old man of Stiles Hall' by California State Legislature resolution.
Reactivated the Society in 1922 after it had lapsed; made honorary member for life.

career.organization: Save the Redwoods League / California Academy of Sciences / Friends of the Bancroft Library

cross_connections.display_label: also a pioneering historian of Gold Rush California

narrative_blocks.header: Grand old man of Stiles Hall, fifty years
narrative_blocks.body: Anson Stiles Blake chaired the board of the University YMCA at UC Berkeley for fifty years — the building, Stiles Hall, took its name from a benefactor in his Stiles family line. The California State Legislature formally named him the 'Grand old man of Stiles Hall' in recognition of that service.

narrative_blocks.header: Reactivated the California Historical Society, 1922
narrative_blocks.body: When the California Historical Society had lapsed, Anson Stiles Blake organized its 1922 revival and served as president. The Society later made him an honorary member for life. He sat on the boards of the Save the Redwoods League, California Academy of Sciences, and Friends of the Bancroft Library.

narrative_blocks.header: Family quarry firm at Richmond, California
narrative_blocks.body: Anson presided over Blake Brothers Co., the family quarry business at Richmond, California — extending into industrial materials a Blake-family talent for working stone that had begun with his grandfather Eli Whitney Blake's invention of the stone crusher in the 1850s.

burial.plot_notes: Memorial shared with husband Sherman Day Thacher.

education.notes: Graduated with A.B. degree from the state university in San Francisco/Berkeley in 1895.

institutions.notes: Raised six surviving children on the Thacher School campus from 1896.

narrative_blocks.header: Three infants lost in three years, 1898-1901
narrative_blocks.body: Between her firstborn Elizabeth (1897) and her son George (1903), Eliza Seely Blake Thacher lost three children in infancy: two unnamed sons in 1898 and 1900, and Charles Thacher in 1901. After this four-year cluster of grief she went on to bear six more children, all of whom survived to adulthood — among them Anson Stiles Thacher, who would later become headmaster of his father's Thacher School.

burial.plot_notes: Ashes returned to New Haven; memorial shared with wife Eliza.

notable.notable_blurb: Founded The Thacher School in Ojai California, 1889

education.notes: Hopkins Grammar School, New Haven CT — preparatory education with rigorous classical curriculum emphasizing Latin and Greek. Graduated 1879.
Won Townsend Prize for speaking at graduation and the John A. Porter Prize for thesis on 'Municipal Government.'

career.notes: Brief private legal practice after Yale Law graduation; left for California in 1887 to accompany his ailing younger brother George.

career.organization: Casa de Piedra Ranch, Ojai Valley
career.notes: Homesteaded 160 acres at Casa de Piedra ranch near Nordhoff (now Ojai). Lived and ran the school here until his death.
Founded 1889 by tutoring one student from the East on his ranch; school grew to ~60 boarders by the early 1920s. Headmaster for 42 years until retirement six weeks before death.
Local public school district board service for 14 years.

career.organization: Nordhoff High School Board of Trustees
career.notes: Helped shape local public secondary education for 14 years.
Civic leadership in the Ojai Valley.

career.organization: Edward Drummond Libbey civic improvement projects
career.notes: Collaborated with Toledo glass philanthropist Edward Drummond Libbey on downtown Ojai's Spanish-revival beautification — the project that transformed the ranch settlement of Nordhoff into the planned Spanish-revival town of Ojai still visible today.

career.organization: Ojai Valley Presbyterian Church (now Community Church)
career.notes: Joined 1887; remained active throughout life including service on the building committee. Lifelong Presbyterian.

institutions.notes: Founded the school on his Casa de Piedra ranch in 1889; headmaster until retirement six weeks before death in 1931.

naming_inspiration.notes: First name 'Sherman' carries his mother Elizabeth Baldwin Sherman's family surname, traced to Roger Sherman the signer (great-grandfather). The Sherman family of New Haven was deeply Yale-connected — 'Sherman' became a given name in the Thacher branch as a marker of that pedigree.

narrative_blocks.header: Began a school for a friend's son, 1889
narrative_blocks.body: When a New England friend asked Sherman Day Thacher to tutor his son for Yale admission, Thacher agreed only if the boy could come to Ojai. The friend agreed, and Mr. Thacher's School began in 1889 — known today as The Thacher School, one of California's oldest boarding schools.

narrative_blocks.header: Came west in 1887 for his ailing brother
narrative_blocks.body: Sherman accompanied his ailing younger brother George Thacher — who had tuberculosis — from New Haven to the Ojai Valley in 1887, where the family hoped the climate would help. Their half-brother Edward Stanley Thacher had already pioneered orange farming in the same valley. Sherman intended the trip as temporary but stayed for the rest of his life.

narrative_blocks.header: Hopkins, Yale, then mountain trails as classrooms
narrative_blocks.body: Sherman's own education at Hopkins Grammar School and Yale was strict and classical — scholar-teachers, seated meals in coat and tie. The school he founded in Ojai inverted that model, turning California mountains and meadows into classrooms with horses as some of his best teachers.

narrative_blocks.header: Operative shock killed him, six weeks after retirement
narrative_blocks.body: Sherman Day Thacher announced his retirement as headmaster at the 1931 commencement, deferring to his physicians' recommendations after declining health. He died six weeks later on August 5, 1931, age 69, of operative shock — and his ashes were returned to Grove Street Cemetery in New Haven, the cemetery of his great-grandfather Roger Sherman the signer.

narrative_blocks.header: Mandatory equestrian program from founding day
narrative_blocks.body: From The Thacher School's 1889 founding, Sherman required every boy to care for and ride his own horse — a Western ethos contrasting sharply with the sedentary Eastern boarding schools he had known at Hopkins and Yale. His often-quoted explanation: "There's something about the outside of a horse that's good for the inside of a boy." The requirement still defines the school more than 130 years later.

narrative_blocks.header: Worked with Libbey on downtown Ojai's beautification
narrative_blocks.body: Sherman collaborated with Toledo glass philanthropist Edward Drummond Libbey on the 1910s downtown Ojai beautification project. The collaboration transformed the small ranch settlement of Nordhoff into the planned Spanish-revival town of Ojai — the architectural identity it carries today.

quotes.text: There's something about the outside of a horse that's good for the inside of a boy.
quotes.context: Sherman Day Thacher's famous aphorism justifying the Thacher School's founding-day requirement that every student care for a horse. Widely quoted in coverage of the school for over a century.
quotes.source_label: Quoted in The Thacher School Wikipedia article and the LA Times obituary of his son Anson Stiles Thacher (Aug 24, 1994).

bio.photo_notes: Group photo of five Thacher siblings.

narrative_blocks.header: Thacher died young
narrative_blocks.body: Thacher died in 1898, in his first year. His parents gave the name to a brother born two years later.

narrative_blocks.header: Thacher died young
narrative_blocks.body: Thacher died in 1900, in his first year.

narrative_blocks.header: Charles died young
narrative_blocks.body: Charles died in 1901, in his first year.

bio.photo_notes: Couple portrait with wife Helena Duryea Thacher (I00598).

career.notes: 25-year career building hydroelectric projects on the western slopes of the Sierra Nevadas from Shasta County to Kings County.
Ran the family quarry products company for several years after first retirement — same firm uncle Anson Stiles Blake (HD4106) had presided over.

career.organization: San Francisco Bay Area Rapid Transit (BART)
career.notes: Consulting engineer for a Bay Area rapid transit tunnel after second retirement.

cross_connections.display_label: Yale science PhDs through different Blake siblings
cross_connections.notes: HD4101 (great-grandson of HD1556 Eliza Maria O'Brien Blake via Charles Thompson Blake) and HD4112 (grandson of HD1556 via Frances Louisa Blake) — Yale PhD math/physics 1925 vs Yale PhD chemistry 1885. Two Hooker descendants in the Blake-Whitney scientific tradition.

narrative_blocks.header: Sierra Nevada hydropower across twenty-five years
narrative_blocks.body: George Blake Thacher spent 25 years at PG&E as resident engineer on hydroelectric projects strung across the western slopes of the Sierra Nevadas — from Shasta County in the north to Kings County in the south. He raised his sons Philip and John in the mountains while building dams.

narrative_blocks.header: BART tunnel consultant after second retirement
narrative_blocks.body: Twice retired — first from PG&E's hydropower projects, then from running the family Blake Brothers Co. quarry in Richmond — George was asked back as consulting engineer for a Bay Area Rapid Transit tunnel. The Yale-and-MIT-trained engineer's final professional act was helping cut the underground transit lines of the modern Bay Area.

bio.photo_notes: Group photo of five Thacher siblings.

education.notes: Student at his father's school where he was born on campus; graduated CdeP 1923 (Casa de Piedra Class of 1923 — the school's class-year designation honoring its founding ranch).

career.notes: Taught at the prestigious Massachusetts prep school.
Taught at the Catalina Island School before returning home to Thacher.
Taught mathematics at the school his father founded; 39-year tenure through 1970 retirement.
Headmaster of the oldest private boarding school west of the Mississippi for 13 years.
30+ year tenure.
30+ year tenure.

institutions.notes: Born on campus; taught at the school 1931-1970; headmaster 1936-1949.

narrative_blocks.header: Born on campus, taught there nearly forty years
narrative_blocks.body: Anson Stiles Thacher was born on the Thacher School campus where his father had founded the school sixteen years earlier, graduated CdeP 1923, then went to Yale and taught at Groton and Catalina Island School. He returned home in 1931 and taught mathematics at his father's school until his 1970 retirement — a 39-year tenure as student-turned-master at the same institution.

narrative_blocks.header: Headmaster of his father's school, 1936-1949
narrative_blocks.body: Anson became headmaster of the Thacher School in 1936, twelve years after his father Sherman's 1931 death and after a five-year interregnum under Morgan Barnes. He led the school for thirteen years and was the last member of the Thacher family ever to head the school; under him the student body expanded from Sherman's ~60-boy cap to 94 boys.

narrative_blocks.header: Civic leader of Ojai across four institutions
narrative_blocks.body: In addition to teaching, Anson served as president of the Ojai Civic Association, Ojai Chamber of Commerce, the Senior Canyon Mutual Water Co. (30+ years), and the Ojai Valley School District Board of Trustees. He and his wife Margaret were honored as grand marshals of Ojai's 1989 Independence Day parade.

bio.photo_notes: Group photo of five Thacher siblings.
Group photo of five Thacher siblings.

institutions.notes: Sherman Day "Rusty" Thacher Jr; ninth child of the founder.

narrative_blocks.header: Born Christmas Day 1913, last of nine children
narrative_blocks.body: Sherman Day 'Rusty' Thacher Jr. was born December 25, 1913 in Ventura County, California — the ninth and last child of Sherman Day Thacher Sr. and Eliza Seely Blake. Three of his elder siblings had died in infancy between 1898 and 1901; the surviving six all lived to adulthood.

narrative_blocks.header: Walter died young
narrative_blocks.body: Walter died in 1879 at the age of two.

narrative_blocks.header: Henrietta died young
narrative_blocks.body: Henrietta died in 1883 at the age of one.

notable.notable_blurb: American Byzantinist; scholar of Armenian and Georgian cultures

education.notes: Fellowship years primarily spent in Russia studying Russian, Arabic, Syriac, Armenian, and Georgian languages.

career.notes: Helped found Tbilisi State University in 1918 and taught Greek language and Byzantine history there through 1920.
Instrumental in promoting Byzantine studies in the United States. Contributed to the study of medieval Georgian manuscripts found at Palestine and Mount Athos.

narrative_blocks.header: Lived the Russian Revolution at Tbilisi, 1918
narrative_blocks.body: Robert Pierpont Blake was already in Russia on a Harvard fellowship when the Revolution broke; he relocated to Tbilisi in 1918 on behalf of Saint Petersburg State University to investigate Tbilisi's manuscripts and the texts of the Bible. When Tbilisi State University was founded that same year he became one of its first professors, teaching Greek and Byzantine history through 1920.

narrative_blocks.header: Mount Athos, Palestine, and the Georgian manuscripts
narrative_blocks.body: Robert was instrumental in establishing Byzantine studies in the United States from his Harvard professorship beginning in 1920. He revealed and catalogued many medieval Georgian manuscripts at Palestine and Mount Athos, becoming a foundational figure in modern Byzantine and Caucasian-Christian scholarship.

narrative_blocks.header: Married in Tiflis as Soviet power consolidated, 1920
narrative_blocks.body: On January 7, 1920 Robert married Nadezhda Nikolavena Kryzhanovskaya in Tiflis, Georgia — months before the Soviets absorbed independent Georgia. The couple soon left for Harvard, where Robert would teach for thirty years.

notable.notable_blurb: Namesake of Stiles Hall at UC Berkeley

career.role: Wholesale boot and shoe dealer, then insurance officer
career.notes: Later an officer of several New Haven insurance companies; an original trustee of the University of California.

landmarks.notes: Stiles Hall named in his memorial.

narrative_blocks.header: Berkeley's Stiles Hall bears his name
narrative_blocks.body: After his death the University YMCA at Berkeley, founded in 1884, took his name in memorial, and his widow gave its first building in 1892. His grandson Anson Stiles Blake would chair the hall's board for fifty years.

narrative_blocks.header: His Stiles name ran through three generations
narrative_blocks.body: The Stiles name carried to two grandsons, Anson Stiles Blake and Anson Stiles Thacher. Blake chaired the Berkeley hall's board for half a century, extending the family's tie to it.

notable.notable_blurb: Donated original Stiles Hall building, UC Berkeley, 1892

narrative_blocks.header: Donated Stiles Hall to UC Berkeley, 1892
narrative_blocks.body: Ann Jane Waters Stiles donated the original Romanesque Revival building that became Stiles Hall at UC Berkeley in 1892, in memorial to her late husband Anson Gale Stiles. The building housed the University YMCA on the bank of Strawberry Creek; her grandson Anson Stiles Blake would chair its board for the next fifty years.
